text: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, you want real micronutrient value, not just green vibes.
5.8g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
1.3g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin k at 63% daily value, vitamin c at 40% daily value stand out most. that gives this food real vitamin support.
minerals are basically not part of the case here.

the upsides first: makes meals feel more balanced. the category has real value beyond single-nutrient stats. easy to build meals around. a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. helps add volume without much calorie cost. that makes vegetables easier to use often.

now the downsides: freshness and prep can matter. practical friction is still a real downside. rarely carries a meal by itself. that is normal here, but still part of the tradeoff. usually needs pairing to make a full meal. most vegetables are not complete by themselves.
A tier. Best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
